heroOverview: Coma is a state of prolonged unconsciousness in which a person cannot be awakened, cannot respond purposefully to stimuli, and does not initiate voluntary actions. Unlike normal sleep, coma is not reversed by ordinary arousal stimuli. It arises when the brain's arousal systems — located in the reticular activating system of the brainstem and its projections to the thalamus and cortex — are disrupted by structural injury, metabolic derangement, or toxic exposure. Roughly 50-60% of coma cases in hospital emergency settings are attributed to diffuse metabolic or toxic causes, while structural causes such as stroke or traumatic brain injury account for most of the remainder. Outcomes range from full recovery to persistent vegetative state or death, depending on the underlying cause, depth of coma, and speed of treatment. Rapid identification of a reversible cause is the single most important determinant of outcome.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1-2% of all emergency department presentations involve impaired consciousness or coma; exact incidence varies by definition and setting
keyStats.demographics: Occurs at all ages; traumatic causes peak in young adults (15-35 years); metabolic and vascular causes predominate in adults over 60
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal — peaks in young adults (trauma) and older adults (stroke, metabolic)
keyStats.globalCases: Non-traumatic coma affects an estimated 7-10 per 100,000 population annually in high-income countries; global data are limited

definition: Coma (ICD-10: R40.20) is a clinical state of unarousable unresponsiveness lasting more than one hour, in which the eyes remain closed and the patient shows no purposeful response to external stimuli or inner need. It is distinct from sleep, which reverses with arousal, and from sedation, which reverses with drug elimination. The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is the most widely used assessment tool: a score of 8 or below defines coma for clinical purposes. Coma arises from dysfunction of one of three anatomical substrates — bilateral cerebral hemispheres, the brainstem reticular activating system, or diffuse metabolic disruption of both. Structural coma is caused by mass lesions, haemorrhage, infarction, or herniation. Metabolic coma results from hypoglycaemia, hyperglycaemia, hepatic failure, uraemia, hypoxia, or electrolyte disturbances. Toxic coma follows exposure to drugs, alcohol, carbon monoxide, or other poisons. In ICD-10 terminology, unspecified coma (R40.20) is coded when the cause has not yet been determined at the time of coding. The condition falls under general medicine and intensive care, with specialist input from neurology, neurosurgery, and toxicology depending on cause.

typesClassification.description: Caused by a physical lesion in the brain — such as stroke, subdural haematoma, tumour, or herniation — that directly compresses or destroys arousal pathways.
Results from systemic disturbances such as hypoglycaemia, liver failure, severe sepsis, drug overdose, or hypoxia that globally impair neuronal function without a focal lesion.
Follows head injury with diffuse axonal injury, intracranial bleeding, or cerebral oedema; graded by GCS at presentation and duration.
Occurs after cardiac arrest or prolonged hypoxaemia; neurons in the hippocampus and cortex are selectively vulnerable, and outcome depends on the duration of oxygen deprivation.

primarySymptoms: Complete failure to open the eyes spontaneously or in response to verbal commands or pain — a defining feature scored on the GCS.
Absence of any meaningful verbal output, ranging from no sounds at all to only incomprehensible moaning.
No purposeful movement of the limbs; patients may show only reflexive posturing (decerebrate or decorticate) or flaccid unresponsiveness.
Loss of all normal sleep–wake cycling, so the person lies motionless without transitioning between sleep and wakefulness.
Absent or abnormal protective reflexes, including failure to cough, gag, or respond to corneal touch, depending on depth.
Abnormal breathing patterns — Cheyne-Stokes respiration, central neurogenic hyperventilation, or apnoeic breathing — reflecting brainstem dysfunction.
Pupillary abnormalities: fixed dilated pupils suggest uncal herniation or drug toxicity; pinpoint pupils suggest opioid or pontine lesion; unequal pupils suggest a unilateral structural cause.
Abnormal eye movements — conjugate deviation, dysconjugate gaze, or absent doll's eye and caloric responses — localising the lesion to the brainstem.
Autonomic instability: hyperthermia, bradycardia, hypertension (Cushing's response), or hypothermia depending on cause.
Urinary and faecal incontinence due to loss of cortical control over bladder and bowel sphincters.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of severe headache (worst ever), which may precede coma from subarachnoid haemorrhage by minutes to hours.
Progressive drowsiness, confusion, or failure to recognise familiar people, suggesting rising intracranial pressure or metabolic deterioration.
Focal neurological signs — arm weakness, facial asymmetry, speech difficulty — heralding a structural event.
Seizure activity, which can be followed immediately by post-ictal unresponsiveness or can itself be the harbinger of structural disease.
emergencySigns: No response to voice or pain — call emergency services immediately.
Irregular or absent breathing alongside unconsciousness — begin CPR if no pulse is detectable.
Unequal pupils or one fixed and dilated pupil, indicating possible brain herniation requiring neurosurgical emergency.
Decerebrate posturing (extension of arms and legs) — a sign of deep brainstem dysfunction.
Trauma to the head, neck, or spine combined with unconsciousness — immobilise the cervical spine and call emergency services.

causes.description: Ischaemic stroke to the brainstem or bilateral hemispheres, or haemorrhagic stroke with mass effect, can destroy or compress the reticular activating system. Haemorrhagic stroke causes coma within minutes due to direct tissue destruction and raised intracranial pressure.

High-energy trauma produces diffuse axonal injury, contusion, subdural or epidural haematoma, and cerebral oedema. The combination of direct structural damage and secondary swelling drives coma; GCS measured at 6 hours post-injury is a validated outcome predictor.

Blood glucose below approximately 2.2 mmol/L deprives neurons of their obligate fuel, causing rapid dysfunction of the cerebral cortex and ultimately the brainstem. Hypoglycaemic coma is reversible with prompt glucose administration, making it one of the most important diagnoses to exclude in any unresponsive patient.

causes.cause: Hepatic encephalopathy and acute liver failure
causes.description: Failure of the liver to clear ammonia and other neurotoxins leads to astrocyte swelling and neuronal dysfunction. Grade IV hepatic encephalopathy is characterised by coma and cerebral oedema and carries high mortality without transplantation.

causes.cause: Drug or alcohol intoxication and overdose
causes.description: CNS depressants including opioids, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, and ethanol suppress brainstem arousal centres in a dose-dependent fashion. Opioid-induced coma is rapidly reversible with naloxone, which is why recognising the pinpoint pupils and respiratory depression is critical.

Each minute of cardiac arrest without CPR reduces the probability of neurologically intact survival by approximately 10%. Global cerebral ischaemia produces coma that persists after return of spontaneous circulation; targeted temperature management is used to limit secondary injury.

Bacterial meningitis with severe cerebral oedema, or viral or autoimmune encephalitis involving the brainstem and limbic system, can progress to coma within hours. Immediate empirical antibiotics are given without waiting for confirmatory results.

Hyponatraemia below 120 mmol/L, hypercapnia from respiratory failure, severe uraemia, and thyroid storm or myxoedema coma each suppress global neuronal function through distinct mechanisms, and all are potentially reversible with treatment.

riskFactors.description: A history of atrial fibrillation, carotid disease, or prior stroke raises the risk of a further vascular event that can produce coma, particularly in adults over 65.
Both severe hypoglycaemia (from insulin or sulphonylurea excess) and hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state can lead to coma; tight but not excessive glucose control reduces risk substantially.

riskFactors.factor: Alcohol use disorder and substance use
riskFactors.description: Chronic heavy alcohol use predisposes to intoxication coma, Wernicke encephalopathy, subdural haematoma from falls, and hepatic encephalopathy, while illicit drug or polypharmacy use raises overdose risk.
Impaired hepatic and renal clearance of toxins and drugs lowers the threshold for metabolic coma; managing the underlying disease and adjusting drug doses reduce risk.
Prolonged or serial seizures (status epilepticus) and the post-ictal state produce coma; optimal seizure control with anti-epileptic medication is the primary preventive measure.
Older adults have reduced physiological reserve, more comorbidities, and greater risk of both vascular and metabolic causes of impaired consciousness.

riskFactors.factor: Head trauma risk — occupation or sport
riskFactors.description: Activities with high fall or impact risk increase traumatic brain injury and subdural haematoma likelihood; protective headgear and fall-prevention strategies reduce this risk.
Impaired immunity from HIV, chemotherapy, or immunosuppressive drugs raises the risk of CNS infections — meningitis, encephalitis, cerebral abscess — that can cause coma.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 (predominantly vascular and metabolic causes)
People with diabetes, especially those on insulin or sulphonylureas
Patients with chronic liver disease or alcohol use disorder
People who use opioids or other CNS-depressant drugs
Patients with known epilepsy, particularly during status epilepticus
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing coma begins simultaneously with resuscitation — the ABCDE (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) framework governs the initial assessment. The Glasgow Coma Scale (E1-4, V1-5, M1-6) is scored within the first minutes; a GCS of 8 or below defines coma and mandates airway protection. A bedside glucose measurement takes under 60 seconds and must be done immediately in every unresponsive patient because hypoglycaemia is immediately reversible and universally treatable with intravenous dextrose. Pupillary examination, fundoscopy, and cranial nerve testing localise the lesion: bilateral fixed dilated pupils suggest herniation or drug toxicity; pinpoint pupils suggest opioids or a pontine lesion; papilloedema confirms raised intracranial pressure. History from witnesses — time of onset, preceding symptoms, trauma, medications, alcohol, or drug use — is gathered simultaneously. Blood tests assess glucose, sodium, ammonia, renal and liver function, thyroid function, a toxicology screen, blood cultures, and inflammatory markers. Non-contrast CT of the head is the next immediate step when there is any possibility of a structural cause; it is fast, widely available, and detects haemorrhage, mass lesions, and herniation. CT angiography or MRI follows if the CT is unrevealing. Lumbar puncture is performed if CNS infection is suspected and CT shows no contraindication. EEG is essential when non-convulsive status epilepticus is suspected, particularly in a comatose patient without an obvious structural cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised bedside scoring of eye opening, verbal response, and motor response to quantify the depth of impaired consciousness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician tests your response to voice and then to a painful stimulus; the three scores are added for a total of 3-15, with 8 or below indicating coma.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Immediate exclusion of hypoglycaemia, the most rapidly reversible cause of coma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fingertip blood drop is placed on a glucose strip and read within 30 seconds at the bedside; if glucose is low, intravenous dextrose is given immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects haemorrhage, mass lesion, cerebral oedema, and herniation, guiding urgent neurosurgical or stroke intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless scan taking 2-5 minutes; you lie still while the machine takes cross-sectional X-ray images; performed within 10-20 minutes of coma onset in most hospitals.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood panel and toxicology screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies metabolic and toxic causes — glucose, sodium, ammonia, renal and liver tests, thyroid function, blood and urine drug levels, blood cultures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from a vein in the arm; urine is collected via catheter; results take 30-90 minutes depending on the test.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects meningitis, encephalitis, or subarachnoid haemorrhage not visible on CT by examining cerebrospinal fluid pressure, cells, protein, and glucose.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A needle is inserted between lower lumbar vertebrae while you lie on your side; the procedure takes about 15-20 minutes; only performed after CT excludes raised pressure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects non-convulsive status epilepticus, diffuse encephalopathy patterns, or brain death — conditions that cannot be diagnosed clinically without continuous EEG.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the scalp with paste; recording typically runs for 20-30 minutes at minimum and continuously in ICU monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides superior tissue resolution for detecting diffuse axonal injury, hypoxic injury, encephalitis, and lesions not visible on CT.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan in a narrow tube that is loud; sedation or anaesthesia may be required for unresponsive patients to prevent movement artefact.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of coma is directed at the underlying cause while simultaneously stabilising the airway, breathing, and circulation and preventing secondary brain injury. The universal initial steps are securing a definitive airway with endotracheal intubation (GCS ≤ 8 or loss of airway protective reflexes), giving 100% oxygen, obtaining IV access, checking glucose, and administering intravenous dextrose immediately if glucose is low. Naloxone (0.4-2 mg IV or IM) is given empirically if opioid toxicity is suspected, with dramatic awakening confirming the diagnosis. Thiamine 100 mg IV is given before or with dextrose in any malnourished or alcohol-dependent patient to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics plus IV aciclovir are started immediately if CNS infection cannot be excluded. Raised intracranial pressure is managed with head elevation to 30°, avoidance of hypercapnia, osmotherapy with mannitol or hypertonic saline, and neurosurgical consultation for surgical decompression when indicated. Hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, and toxic exposures each have specific antidotes or correction protocols. Neuroprognosis after cardiac arrest is systematically evaluated at 72 hours using a multimodal approach: GCS motor score, pupillary light reflex, somatosensory evoked potentials, EEG, and brain MRI. Throughout the acute phase, intensive care nursing prevents complications: aspiration pneumonia, venous thromboembolism, pressure sores, and contractures. Families need clear, accurate prognostic information at each stage.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous dextrose for hypoglycaemia
medicalTreatments.description: 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV is given immediately for glucose below 3.5 mmol/L in any unresponsive patient. It is the most reliably curative intervention in coma medicine — patients with hypoglycaemic coma treated within 30 minutes typically recover fully.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near 100% reversal of coma when hypoglycaemia is the sole cause and glucose is corrected within 30-60 minutes.

medicalTreatments.name: Naloxone for opioid toxicity
medicalTreatments.description: An opioid receptor antagonist given IV, IM, or intranasally at 0.4-2 mg, reversing opioid-induced CNS and respiratory depression; repeat doses or an infusion may be needed for long-acting opioids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reverses opioid coma in approximately 95% of cases within 2-5 minutes; duration 30-90 minutes, shorter than many opioids.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics plus aciclovir for CNS infection
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical IV ceftriaxone plus dexamethasone for bacterial meningitis, and IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for herpes encephalitis, are started before confirmatory results because a delay of even one hour worsens outcome in bacterial meningitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality from bacterial meningitis falls from over 50% untreated to approximately 20-30% with early antibiotics; herpes encephalitis mortality falls from over 70% to approximately 20% with aciclovir.

medicalTreatments.name: Osmotherapy for raised intracranial pressure
medicalTreatments.description: Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 minutes or 30 mL boluses of 23.4% hypertonic saline reduce cerebral oedema by osmotic gradient, buying time for definitive surgical or medical treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mannitol reduces ICP by an average of 5-15 mmHg within 20-30 minutes in approximately 70-80% of patients; effect lasts 1-4 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Targeted temperature management after cardiac arrest
medicalTreatments.description: Cooling to 32-36°C for 24 hours after return of spontaneous circulation limits secondary neuronal injury from ischaemia-reperfusion; combined with careful glucose control, CO2 targeting, and seizure monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves rate of favourable neurological outcome by approximately 10-20 percentage points compared with no temperature control in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest survivors in multiple trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-epileptic drugs for status epilepticus
medicalTreatments.description: IV lorazepam or diazepam first-line, followed by IV levetiracetam, valproate, or fosphenytoin second-line, aiming to terminate seizures within 30-60 minutes; refractory cases require anaesthetic infusions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: First-line benzodiazepines terminate status epilepticus in approximately 50-60% of cases; combined first- and second-line therapy achieves control in approximately 80%.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical evacuation of haematoma
surgicalOptions.description: Craniotomy or burr-hole drainage of subdural, epidural, or intracerebral haematoma relieves mass effect and restores cerebral perfusion; timing is guided by clot volume, GCS trajectory, and CT findings.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Favourable outcomes in roughly 60-70% of patients with acute subdural haematoma when operated within 4 hours of neurological deterioration; outcomes drop sharply with delay.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive craniectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of a large bone flap to allow the swollen brain to expand outward rather than herniate downward through the tentorium; used in large malignant hemispheric infarction or severe traumatic oedema.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces mortality from malignant MCA infarction by approximately 50% but increases the proportion of survivors with moderate-to-severe disability; patient selection is critical.

surgicalOptions.name: Placement of intracranial pressure monitor
surgicalOptions.description: An intraventricular catheter or parenchymal probe measures ICP continuously, allowing pressure-guided osmotherapy and cerebral perfusion pressure optimisation in traumatic and post-cardiac-arrest coma.
surgicalOptions.successRate: ICP-guided management has been associated with approximately 12-15 mmHg lower average ICP and improved outcome in traumatic brain injury in observational series, though the BEST:TRIP RCT showed no mortality benefit over clinical monitoring alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive and preventive nursing care
alternativeTreatments.description: Meticulous positioning, regular turns to prevent pressure sores, passive range-of-motion exercises to prevent contractures, and oral hygiene are cornerstones of coma care in the ICU and subacute setting.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sensory stimulation programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured programmes exposing patients with prolonged disorders of consciousness to familiar voices, music, and tactile stimuli may modestly accelerate recovery in some patients; evidence from randomised trials is limited.

whySeeSpecialist: Coma is managed in intensive care from the outset, but specialist neurological and neurosurgical input is essential. A neurologist guides the assessment of non-convulsive seizures, metabolic encephalopathy, and neuro-prognostication. A neurosurgeon is needed when structural lesions require urgent evacuation. Toxicology consultation supports complex poisonings. For prolonged disorders of consciousness beyond the acute phase, a rehabilitation medicine specialist coordinates recovery planning, family communication, and assessment for post-acute programmes.
doctorSelectionGuide: In the acute setting, care is directed by emergency medicine and intensive care physicians who initiate resuscitation and organ support. A neurologist with expertise in coma, EEG monitoring, and neurocritical care should be involved early. For patients who survive the acute phase with persistent impairment, a neurorehabilitation team — including specialist physicians, neuropsychologists, speech therapists, and physiotherapists — oversees the transition to rehabilitation and provides families with realistic, evidence-based prognostic information.
preventionStrategies: Control cardiovascular risk factors — hypertension, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia — to reduce stroke risk, the most common vascular cause of coma.
Wear helmets and seatbelts consistently; helmets reduce serious traumatic brain injury by 60-80% in cyclists and motorcyclists.
Monitor blood glucose closely in people on insulin or sulphonylureas; carry glucose tablets; set continuous glucose monitor alerts.
Treat epilepsy with adequate anti-epileptic medication and avoid precipitants of breakthrough seizures.
Seek treatment for alcohol use disorder and avoid polysubstance combinations that depress the CNS.
Follow up chronic liver or kidney disease to detect deterioration before encephalopathy develops.
lifestyleModifications: After recovery, adhere to prescribed anticoagulation, antiplatelet, or anti-epileptic regimens, which are the most important secondary prevention steps.
Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery until formally assessed as safe; legal requirements vary by country and cause.
Engage in supervised cognitive and physical rehabilitation; structured programmes improve functional recovery after brain injury.
Reduce fall risk at home with grab rails, non-slip mats, and adequate lighting, particularly for older adults recovering from vascular events.
Maintain good hydration and nutritional intake to support brain recovery and prevent metabolic relapse.

recommended: Adequate caloric intake — ICU patients require approximately 25 kcal/kg/day enterally to prevent catabolism
Adequate protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support muscle preservation during prolonged immobility
Thiamine supplementation in all patients with suspected or confirmed alcohol use disorder
Branched-chain amino acid enriched formulas for patients with hepatic encephalopathy to reduce ammonia burden
avoid: High-protein feeds in uncontrolled hepatic encephalopathy, which worsen ammonia load
Excess free water in hyponatraemia-related coma until sodium is safely corrected
Enteral feeds at high rates without gastric residual monitoring, which raises aspiration risk

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute phase, passive range-of-motion exercises are performed by physiotherapists twice daily to prevent joint contractures and deep vein thrombosis. Passive standing on a tilt table begins as haemodynamic stability allows. Once a patient shows arousal, graded active physiotherapy is introduced. Return to exercise after coma depends entirely on the cause and residual impairment; a neurologist or rehabilitation physician sets safe limits.
dailyManagement: In the ICU: hourly neurological observations including GCS, pupils, and limb movements guide clinical decisions.
Continuous EEG monitoring is maintained in patients with unexplained fluctuating consciousness or prior seizures.
Position the head at 30° elevation to reduce intracranial pressure and aspiration risk.
Provide family updates at least daily using clear, plain language; families experience significant psychological distress and need consistent communication.
Reassess the working diagnosis daily — coma that does not improve or that worsens may indicate a missed treatable cause.
prognosis: The outcome of coma depends almost entirely on its cause and on the speed and appropriateness of treatment. Metabolic and toxic causes — hypoglycaemia, drug overdose, Wernicke encephalopathy — carry the best prognosis when reversed promptly; full neurological recovery is the rule if treatment is fast enough. Traumatic coma has variable outcomes: approximately 50% of patients with a post-resuscitation GCS of 3-8 either die or remain in a vegetative state, while roughly one-third achieve independence. Hypoxic-ischaemic coma after cardiac arrest has the most guarded prognosis; in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, fewer than 10% of patients survive with good neurological function. Structural coma from supratentorial haemorrhage or infarction has outcomes tied closely to lesion volume and the extent of herniation. A GCS score of 3 with absent brainstem reflexes at 72 hours is associated with a very poor outcome in non-metabolic coma. Prolonged disorders of consciousness (vegetative and minimally conscious states) require specialist assessment because a minority of patients, especially those injured by trauma, recover awareness months to years later.
recoveryTimeline: Patients whose coma has a rapidly reversible cause — hypoglycaemia, opioid overdose, hyponatraemia — may awaken within minutes to hours of treatment. Those with traumatic or structural coma typically follow a longer trajectory: GCS improves over days to weeks, with most survivors who will eventually become independent showing meaningful improvement by three months. Hypoxic-ischaemic coma after cardiac arrest is formally prognosticated at 72 hours; patients who remain comatose at that time with absent pupillary reflexes and flat EEG have a very high probability of poor outcome. Recovery of awareness from a vegetative state is possible up to 12 months after trauma (and occasionally longer) but is rare after 3 months for non-traumatic causes.
complications: Aspiration pneumonia — secretions pooling in the immobile patient reach the lungs; the leading cause of death after the acute phase.
Ventilator-associated pneumonia — develops in approximately 10-20% of intubated patients per ICU day; treated with targeted antibiotics.
Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — immobility promotes clot formation; low-molecular-weight heparin prophylaxis and compression stockings are standard.
Pressure sores — prolonged immobility over bony prominences causes ischaemic skin necrosis; two-hourly repositioning is the primary prevention.
Contractures and muscle wasting — weeks of immobility permanently shorten tendons and atrophy muscles without daily passive physiotherapy.
Post-traumatic epilepsy — seizures develop in approximately 5-15% of patients after traumatic brain injury, requiring long-term anti-epileptic therapy.

supportResources.name: NHS — Coma
supportResources.description: UK patient guide to coma signs, causes, treatment, and recovery from the National Health Service.

supportResources.name: International Brain Injury Association
supportResources.description: Supports patients, families, and clinicians dealing with acquired brain injury including coma and disorders of consciousness.

sources.title: Posner JB et al. Plum and Posner's Diagnosis and Treatment of Stupor and Coma, 4th edition. Oxford University Press, 2007
Teasdale G, Jennett B. Assessment of coma and impaired consciousness. The Lancet, 1974
Wijdicks EF et al. Recommendations for the Management of Cerebral and Cerebellar Infarction with Swelling. Stroke, 2014
Nolan JP et al. European Resuscitation Council Guidelines 2021: Post-resuscitation care

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy / post-ictal state
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A post-ictal state after a grand mal seizure can mimic coma but resolves within 10-30 minutes and is preceded by witnessed convulsions, unlike primary coma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stroke causes focal neurological deficits and can cause coma if it affects the brainstem or is very large, but many strokes leave the patient conscious with one-sided weakness only.

confusedWithConditions.name: Advanced dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: End-stage dementia produces profound unresponsiveness that can resemble coma, but it develops over months to years and is distinguished by the chronic history and preserved vegetative cycles.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: One of the most common structural causes of coma; vascular events to the brainstem or large hemispheric strokes with oedema are frequent precipitants.

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Status epilepticus can produce coma, and post-ictal states follow major seizures; coma may also trigger seizures as a complication.

relatedConditions.name: Subdural haematoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute and subacute subdural haematomas are a surgically treatable structural cause of coma, particularly after trauma or in anticoagulated patients.

relatedConditions.name: Brain tumour (glioma)
relatedConditions.relevance: Rapidly growing brain tumours or those causing obstructive hydrocephalus can precipitate coma through raised intracranial pressure or herniation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a coma and being unconscious?
faqs.answer: Coma is a prolonged state of deep unconsciousness lasting more than one hour, where the person cannot be woken and does not respond purposefully to any stimulus. Brief unconsciousness lasting seconds to minutes — as after fainting or a seizure — is not called coma.

faqs.question: Can a person in a coma hear you?
faqs.answer: Some patients in light coma or in a minimally conscious state retain some auditory processing. Talking to a comatose person using familiar voices and comforting words is encouraged, as it causes no harm and may be beneficial. However, no response does not mean the person cannot hear.

faqs.question: What causes coma?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are stroke, traumatic brain injury, severe hypoglycaemia, drug or alcohol overdose, cardiac arrest, meningitis or encephalitis, and metabolic disorders such as liver failure. Roughly half of all coma cases have a metabolic or toxic cause that is potentially reversible.

faqs.question: How is coma measured?
faqs.answer: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) scores eye opening (1-4), verbal response (1-5), and motor response (1-6). A total score of 8 or below defines coma. It takes about one minute to perform at the bedside and guides immediate management decisions.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for coma?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the underlying cause. Immediate steps are securing the airway, checking blood glucose (and giving dextrose if low), giving naloxone for opioid overdose, and starting antibiotics if infection is suspected. Supportive intensive care prevents complications while the brain recovers.

faqs.question: Is coma reversible?
faqs.answer: It depends entirely on the cause. Metabolic and toxic coma — from low blood sugar, opioids, or alcohol — can be fully reversed in minutes with the right treatment. Structural coma from severe stroke or prolonged cardiac arrest may result in permanent impairment or death.

faqs.question: How long can someone be in a coma?
faqs.answer: Duration varies from hours to years. A coma lasting more than four weeks without any sign of awareness is reclassified as a vegetative state. Most patients who will recover regain awareness within the first four weeks for metabolic causes and within three to six months for traumatic causes.

faqs.question: What happens to the brain during coma?
faqs.answer: Coma occurs when the brain's arousal pathways — particularly the brainstem reticular activating system and its connections to the thalamus and cortex — are disrupted. Disruption can be from physical damage, loss of blood supply, metabolic failure, or toxic suppression of neuronal activity.

faqs.question: Can someone wake up from a coma on their own?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly when the cause is metabolic or toxic and is resolving naturally, such as alcohol clearance. However, most serious comas require active treatment of the underlying cause. Spontaneous awakening from deep structural coma without treatment is rare and unpredictable.

faqs.question: What is the Glasgow Coma Scale?
faqs.answer: The Glasgow Coma Scale scores three responses — eye opening, verbal output, and motor movement — each on a numerical scale. The three scores are added; the maximum is 15 (normal) and the minimum is 3 (no response). A score of 8 or below indicates coma requiring airway protection.

faqs.question: What is a medically induced coma?
faqs.answer: A medically induced coma uses IV anaesthetic drugs — typically propofol or midazolam — to deliberately suppress brain activity and reduce intracranial pressure or stop refractory seizures. Unlike pathological coma, it is controlled, monitored, and reversed when the clinical goal is achieved.

faqs.question: How do doctors tell if coma will improve?
faqs.answer: Prognostication uses a combination of factors: GCS motor score, pupillary responses, brainstem reflexes, EEG patterns, somatosensory evoked potentials, and brain MRI. For cardiac arrest, formal multimodal assessment is recommended at 72 hours. Metabolic coma is reassessed after the cause is corrected.

faqs.question: What is a vegetative state?
faqs.answer: A vegetative state is diagnosed when a patient shows arousal — eyes open spontaneously — but no awareness of self or environment. It follows coma lasting more than about four weeks. A small proportion of patients, especially after trauma, regain some awareness months to years later.

faqs.question: What complications can develop during coma?
faqs.answer: Common complications are aspiration pneumonia, pressure sores, deep vein thrombosis, contractures, and muscle wasting. These are prevented with intensive nursing: regular repositioning, chest physiotherapy, prophylactic anticoagulation, and passive limb exercises.

faqs.question: Is coma painful?
faqs.answer: Patients in deep coma are unaware of pain by definition. However, in lighter states of impaired consciousness, pain and distress may be present, which is why adequate analgesia and sedation are maintained in the ICU. Pain assessment tools for non-communicating patients guide dosing.

faqs.question: Can coma be caused by diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe hypoglycaemia — most often from excess insulin — is one of the most common and reversible causes of coma. Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state with very high blood sugar can also cause coma. Both are diabetic emergencies requiring immediate hospital treatment.

faqs.question: What is locked-in syndrome and is it coma?
faqs.answer: Locked-in syndrome is not coma. The patient is fully awake and aware but cannot move or speak because of brainstem paralysis, usually from a pontine stroke. They typically communicate only by vertical eye movements. This distinction is critical and can be confirmed by EEG or eye-tracking assessment.

faqs.question: How do families cope when someone is in a coma?
faqs.answer: Uncertainty and helplessness are the most reported experiences of families. Daily updates from the clinical team, involvement in care decisions, access to a social worker or psychologist, and peer support from other families are all valuable. Many ICUs offer family liaison nurses and regular family meetings.

faqs.question: Does talking to someone in a coma help?
faqs.answer: Familiar voices may provide comfort and there is some evidence that auditory stimulation speeds recovery in minimally conscious patients. Talking to a comatose family member is safe and encouraged. Keep conversation calm and reassuring — avoid distressing topics.

faqs.question: Can alcohol cause a coma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe alcohol intoxication depresses the brainstem, causing coma, respiratory depression, and aspiration. A blood alcohol level above approximately 300-400 mg/dL can be fatal without supportive care. Anyone found unconscious after alcohol intake should be placed in the recovery position and emergency services called.

metaDescription: Coma is prolonged unconsciousness caused by brain injury, stroke, low blood sugar, or overdose. Learn GCS scoring, emergency steps, and recovery outlook.
